title: Electrochemical studies of poly(vinylferrocene) films in aqueous and non-aqueous electrolyte solutions
abstract:
Electrochemical studies of poly(vinylferrocene)
ﬁlms in aqueous and non-aqueous electrolyte
solutions
WS Schlindwein,* A Kavvada, RJ Latham and RG Linford
Department of Chemistry, De Montfort University, The Gateway, Leicester, LE1 9BH, UK
Abstract: The electrochemical response of a conducting polymer, poly(vinylferrocene) (PVF), as a
function of the electrolyte media, is reported. Cyclic voltammetry has been used to investigate the
redox behaviour of the ®lms spin-cast onto glass electrodes (ITO). The ®lms were cycled in non-
aqueous (propylene carbonate and tetramethylene sulfone) electrolyte solutions containing different
salts: sodium, lithium and tetrabutylammonium perchlorates (NaClO4, LiClO4 and TBAClO4), tetra-
butylammonium hexa¯uorophosphate and tetra¯uoroborate (TBAPF6, TBABF4). Aqueous electro-
lyte solutions of NaClO4 and LiClO4 have also been studied for comparison with the reported literature
data. Some of the criteria for reversible systems have been tested for the different electrolytes used.
Cyclic voltammetry experiments have also been carried out on the same ®lms, after 3 months, using
electrolyte solutions in propylene carbonate and tetramethylene sulfone. The results show that the ®lm
has a `solvent sensitivity' effect, and the charge density changes according to the solvent used. There is
a reversible charge sensivity effect when the ®lms are cycled between propylene carbonate- and
tetramethylene sulfone-based electrolytes.
# 2000 Society of Chemical Industry
Keywords: redox polymers; poly(vinylferrocene); cyclic voltammetry
INTRODUCTION
In the past 20 years, there has been remarkable
progress in the development of a new category of
polymeric materials known as conducting polymers.
Based on their electrical conduction mechanisms these
materials are usually classi®ed into two groups. One
group includes polymers that transport almost exclu-
sively ionic species and they are called polymer
electrolytes or ionic polymers.1 These ionic polymers
can be easily made into ¯exible thin ®lms with large
surface area, this being a key advantage for extended
applications
in
non-aqueous
batteries
and
other
electrochemical devices.2±4
The other group includes polymers where the
transport mechanism is mainly of an electronic nature;
they are called electronically conducting polymers and
include
conjugated
and
redox
polymers.5 These
electronically conducting polymers have been used in
charge storage devices as electrode materials.6±8 They
are electroactive materials and have composition,
physical and chemical properties that change with
oxidation state. Polypyrroles, polythiophenes and
polyanilines are examples of conjugated conducting
polymers.5 They are commonly prepared electro-
chemically as disordered ®lms with randomly distrib-
uted three-dimensional chains and high porosity.
Poly(vinylferrocene) (PVF) is an example of a redox
polymer that contains localized sites which may be
oxidized and reduced. In this case, charge is not
distributed along the polymer chain but is localized at
speci®c pendant (ferrocene) redox sites (Fe2/Fe3).
Each redox site is electronically independent within
one molecular chain, but mixing valencies between
partially oxidized and reduced sites causes electrical
conductivity in the bulk polymer due to electron
hopping between the sites.9 PVF can undergo a
reversible redox reaction and has been used as a
model redox polymer in electrochemical studies.10,11
The oxidation process occurs by removal of electrons
and the simultaneous insertion of anions (Xÿ) from
the electrolyte to balance the charge (Fig 1). Most of
the important applications of redox polymers are
Figure 1. Schematic representation of PVF in the reduced and oxidized
states with the counter anion Xÿ for charge neutrality.
(Received 13 September 1999; revised version received 21 December 1999; accepted 12 January 2000)
* Correspondence to: WS Schlindwein, Department of Chemistry, De Montfort University, The Gateway, Leicester, LE1 9BH, UK
# 2000 Society of Chemical Industry. Polym Int 0959±8103/2000/$30.00
953
Polymer International
Polym Int 49:953±959 (2000)
 associated with switching the electroactive polymer
®lm from the neutral (reduced) state to the doped
(oxidized) state.
The nature and concentration of the electrolyte
solution affect the redox behaviour of PVF polymer
®lm electrodes.12±15 Electrochemical quartz crystal
microbalance (EQCM) studies have shown that at low
electrolyte concentrations (0.1moldmÿ3) the ®lm is
permselective. Under these conditions the cation does
not participate in the redox reaction and the electro-
neutrality is obtained by the insertion of the counter-
ion and solvent. At high electrolyte concentration,
particularly above 1moldmÿ3, the ®lm is non-perm-
selective and there is a volume change associated with
the redox process. It was observed that the volume of
the oxidized PVF ®lm under non-permselective con-
dition shrinks by 35%.13,14
The shape of the cyclic voltammetric anodic peak is
more solvent dependent than the cathodic peak,
particularly for aqueous media. For example, the
anodic peak observed in H2O/LiCLO4 is very narrow
compared with that in acetonitrile.15 The broadening
of the peaks has been attributed to repulsive interac-
tions, differences in spatial distribution of the redox
centres, and a distribution of species with different E0
values for the electroactive groups.10
Kinetic factors involved in the oxidation or reduc-
tion of polymer electrodes are complicated by the fact
that most of the ®xed electroactive sites are spatially
remote from the electrode polymer interface.15 A
210AÊ thick ®lm corresponds to about 20 monolayers
of individual ferrocene units. Consequently, coupled
electron/ion transport within the ®lm may be a
signi®cant factor.
Results on the preparation and characterization by
cyclic voltammetry of PVF ®lms (more than 1500AÊ
thick) in aqueous solutions of 0.1M LiClO4 or
NaClO4, and in non-aqueous (propylene carbonate
(PC) and tetramethylene sulphone (sulfolane)) solu-
tions of 0.1M LiClO4, NaClO4, tetrabutylammonium
perchlorate (TBACIO4), tetrabutylammonium tetra-
¯uoroborate
(TBABF4)
and
tetrabutylammonium
hexa¯uorophosphate (TBAPF6) electrolytes are pre-
sented in this paper.
EXPERIMENTAL
Materials
Poly(vinylferrocene) (Polysciences Inc) was dried
under vacuum at room temperature for 24h and kept
in a desiccator. Tetrabutylammonium perchlorate
(TBAP) (Sigma, 98%), tetrabutylammonium tetra-
¯uoroborate (TBABF4) (Aldrich, 99%), tetrabutyl-
ammonium hexa¯uorophosphate (TBAPF6) (Aldrich,
99%), sodium perchlorate (NaClO4) (Aldrich, 99%)
and lithium perchlorate (LiClO4) (Aldrich, 99.99%)
were recrystallized before use. Propylene carbonate
(PC) (Sigma, 99.7%) was stored over molecular sieves
and tetramethylene sulfone (sulfolane) (Avocado,
99%) was double distilled under vacuum before use.
Dichloromethane (CH2Cl2) (Fisher Scienti®c, HPLC
grade, less than 0.002% of water) was used as
received. ITO electrodes were supplied by Balzers
(resistance about 80Ocmÿ2).
Film preparation
PVF ®lms were spin-cast from a 0.4mM solution of
PVF in CH2Cl2 onto ITO electrodes. The ®lms
produced were, on average, 1600AÊ thick. The thick-
ness was determined by scanning electron microscopy
(SEM) using the cross section of the electrode. The
thickness reproducibility achieved was 95%.
Electrochemical measurements
Staircase cyclic voltammetry experiments were carried
out using the Autolab PGSTAT30 (Windsor Scienti®c
Limited, Slough, UK) with scan rate of 10m Vsÿ1
between 0.0V and 0.6V against saturated calomel
electrode (SCE). The ®lms were cycled in aqueous
solutions of 0.1M LiClO4 and NaClO4 and in non-
aqueous
(PC
and
sulfolane)
solutions
of
0.1M
LiClO4, NaClO4, TBAClO4, TBABF4 and TBAPF6
electrolytes.
The electrochemical cell used consisted of one
compartment connected with a Luggin capillary for
the reference electrode (aqueous SCE). ITO glass was
used as counter and as working electrodes. The ®lms
were cycled 90 times after a pre-conditioning step of
10 cycles to obtain a steady-state response. The
electrochemical responses of electroactive polymer
®lms are frequently observed to differ on the ®rst
(few) and subsequent redox cycles. This phenomenon
was ®rst reported by Kaufman as a `break-in' effect.16
This effect was found in this work and became
negligible after the pre-conditioning step of 10 cycles.
The data analysis was done using the General
Purpose Electrochemical System, (GPES) software,
version 4.6, from Eco Chemie BV. The charge and
current densities were calculated after baseline correc-
tion of the voltammograms.
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
Table 1 shows the effect of the different electrolytes on
the response of PVF ®lms to redox switching in
propylene carbonate. Fig 2 illustrates the changes that
occur from the ®rst (Fig 2a) to the 90th cycle (Fig 2b)
in the voltammograms for the different salts used. The
peak potential Ep is shifted to higher values with the
increase of cation size (Li, Na and TBA) of the
perchlorate salts. An analogous change is less evident
for the different anions ClOÿ
4 ; PFÿ
6 and BFÿ
4 of the TBA
salts. In both cases, the Ep shift also increases with the
number of potential cycles. The peak potential shift, as
a function of number of cycles, could be attributed to
an increase of the ®lm resistance accompanying
structural or morphological changes.
A thermodynamically reversible ®lm should have
the same peak potential during the oxidation and
reduction half cycles. The ®lms should also give

WS Schlindwein et al

butylammonium hexa¯uorophosphate and tetra¯uoroborate (TBAPF6, TBABF4). Aqueous electro-
lyte solutions of NaClO4 and LiClO4 have also been studied for comparison with the reported literature
data. Some of the criteria for reversible systems have been tested for the different electrolytes used.

tetramethylene sulfone-based electrolytes.

Table 1 shows the effect of the different electrolytes on
the response of PVF ®lms to redox switching in
propylene carbonate. Fig 2 illustrates the changes that
occur from the ®rst (Fig 2a) to the 90th cycle (Fig 2b)
in the voltammograms for the different salts used. The
peak potential Ep is shifted to higher values with the
increase of cation size (Li, Na and TBA) of the
perchlorate salts. An analogous change is less evident
salts. In both cases, the Ep shift also increases with the
number of potential cycles. The peak potential shift, as
a function of number of cycles, could be attributed to
an increase of the ®lm resistance accompanying
structural or morphological changes.
A thermodynamically reversible ®lm should have
the same peak potential during the oxidation and
reduction half cycles. The ®lms should also give
current±potential curves that are mirror images of each
other about the current axis. This theoretical beha-
viour was found by Daum and Murray15,17 and by
Peerce and Bard10 for thin PVF ®lms of less than 300AÊ
in acetonitrile electrolyte. The PVF ®lms studied here
do not show this behaviour. They do, however, show
chemical reversibility, because the charge is the same
during oxidation and reduction when only a few cycles
are considered. For many potential cycles this is not
generally the case. There is no evidence of a diffusion
limited reversible process because the current±poten-
tial curves do not meet the criteria for such processes.
For linear sweep and cyclic voltammograms of
diffusion limited reversible reactions, the difference
in potential between the peak potential Ep, and the
potential at half height of the ®rst half cycle (which was
the oxidation cycle) Ep/2, ie (Ep ÿEp/2) should be equal
to 56.6/n, where n is the number of electrons
transferred.17 From Table 1, in the ®rst cycle if we
(EpÿEp/2) is half of the expected value. After 90 cycles
this behaviour changes towards a more diffusion
limited reversible system, with one electron transfer,
Another criterion for diffusion limited reversibility is
that the difference in peak potential is 57mV for a one-
electron transfer reaction.18 Thin ®lms are less likely to
be subject to diffusional limitations. Reversibility of
thin PVF ®lms in LiClO4/acetonitrile electrolytes was
previously observed by Daum and Murray.15 Results
for PVF ®lms over a range of temperatures have been
reported by Chambers and Inzely.19 They have also
shown that thin PVF ®lms (less than 100AÊ thick)
electrolyte have symmetrical wave shapes, indicating
reversibility. Except for TBAPF6, the EpÿEp/2 data
shown in Table 1 are, however, close to 30mV and
they increase to values close to 50mV with the number
A third important diagnostic that can be obtained
from the voltammogram is the ratio between the
anodic and cathodic peak currents (Ip/Ipÿ). For all
kinds of reversible processes, this ratio should be unity
and that is what was found for the perchlorate salts as
shown in Table 1. The peak current ratio, however,
decreases with the number of cycles.
For the ®rst cycle, the charge densities in PC are
approximately 1.2mCcmÿ2 for LiCO4 and TBAClO4
but they are about 40% less for NaClO4 and TBABF4.
The highest charge density in the ®rst cycle was
obtained for TBAPF6. Also, in PC the anodic charge
loss per cycle increases with the cation size for the
perchlorate salts. In TBA salts the charge losses are
similar, but higher than for lithium and sodium
The solvation structure of these electrolytes is
important because of the fundamental role it plays in
Figure 2. Cyclic voltammograms of PVF ﬁlms in propylene carbonate-
based electrolytes: (a) ﬁrst cycles; (b) 90th cycles.
Table 1. Results from the cyclic voltammograms of PVF ﬁlms in propylene carbonate-based electrolytes
TBACIO4 0.327 0.298 0.352 0.300
b Ep/2 is half peak potential.
c Ip is peak current; , Anodic; ÿ, cathodic.
Poly(vinylferrocene) ®lms in electrolyte solutions
ionic mobility. Spectroscopic studies (IR) of LiClO4 in
PC solutions20 have shown the existence of solvent-
separated ion pairs. At low Li concentrations four
solvent molecules were found solvating the ®rst ionic
shell.21 In the case of LiClO4 electrolyte solvents,
decomposition reactions occur readily at the electrode
surface, causing an increase in the local Li concen-
tration in solution.21 Li interacts preferentially with
the oxygen belonging to the carbonyl group of PC.
The oxygen of the solvent carbonyl group is an
electron donor and enters into competition with
4 for association with Li. However, the inter-
action between Li and the carbonyl Oÿ is stronger
and salt dissociation is favoured in dilute solutions.22 If
the cation size is increased, the solvation through the
oxygen from the carbonyl group is less effective and
that is perhaps why the Ep value and the charge loss
increased with cation size, comparing Li and TBA.
The in¯uence of the same salts on the PVF redox
behaviour was also evaluated in sulfolane electrolyte
solutions. The comparative results are presented in
Table 2 and Fig 3 for the different cations, Li, Na
and TBA of the perchlorate salts, and different
potentials Ep are shifted in the same manner as in PC.
In other words, the values increase with the size of the
ions and with the number of cycles. The potential
difference of the anodic and cathodic peaks, however,
does not change as is the case in PC. The values of the
cathodic and anodic peak current are also very similar.
In all cases, except for NaClO4, the systems can be
considered almost chemically reversible with a one
electron transfer process. All the voltammograms in
These results resemble reversible electrochemical pro-
cesses in which the relative activity of Fe2/Fe3 is
uniform throughout the ®lm at each applied electrode
The charge densities in the ®rst cycle are about
0.6mCcmÿ2, except for TBAPF6 which is about
0.35mCcmÿ2. They are, however, almost 50% less
compared with those from the PC electrolytes. In
contrast, the charge loss per cycle is 50% less than in
PC electrolytes, and indeed for the TBAPF6 the
rationalization for this phenomenon would start with
the assumption that the PVF ®lm in sulfolane is very
compact in some regions, and that ion diffusion is slow
in these regions. The gradual swelling of these
compact regions accompanying the repeated oxidation
and reduction of PVF could increase the number of
available redox sites at a particular scan rate.
The solubility of these electrolyte salts in water is
limited; consequently LiClO4 and NaClO4 were used
in aqueous solutions. The results obtained for the
aqueous solutions are presented in Table 3 and Fig 4.
In these cases, the peak potentials Ep are shifted to
lower values with the increase of the cation size of the
perchlorate salts (Li and Na). There is almost no
change in the potential difference of the redox peaks
with the number of cycles, and in the case of LiClO4
Figure 3. Cyclic voltammograms of PVF ﬁlms in sulfolane based
electrolytes: (a) ﬁrst cycles; (b) 90th cycles.
Table 2. Results from the cyclic voltammograms of PVF ﬁlms in sulfolane based electrolytesa
TBACIO4 0.381 0.337 0.410 0.361
there is no charge loss per cycle after 90 cycles. The
stability of the PVF ®lms as a function of number of
cycles is good in aqueous LiClO4 but the redox process
is not reversible. The behaviour of PVF ®lms in
aqueous electrolytes was demonstrated by Daum and
Murray15 to be qualitatively consistent with resistance
phenomena (ohmic resistance effects on peak separa-
tion). The ®lms studied in this work in aqueous
LiClO4 and NaClO4 solutions both have non-symme-
trical oxidation and reduction peaks (Fig 4) and the
peak separation is in the range 100±120mV.
The different characteristics of the PVF ®lms in
different solvents and salts could be interpreted as due
to swelling and or unswelling during the redox process,
different levels of ®lm solvation, and possibly with
phase formation within the ®lm, with consequent
changes in the ®lms' structure±property relationships.
An unusual solvent effect was observed in the ®lms
cycled previously in LiClO4/PC and TBAClO4/sulfo-
lane electrolyte solutions. These ®lms were stored in a
desiccator for about 3 months and subsequently cycled
successively in PC/sulfolane/PC (in that order) LiClO4
electrolytes, then in sulfolane/PC/sulfolane (in that
order) TBAClO4 electrolytes, respectively.
After 3 months, there was a charge loss of 57% for
the ®lm initially cycled in LiClO4/PC and 11% for the
®lm initially cycled in TBAClO4/sulfolane. The results
shown in Fig 5(a,b) indicate that there is a similar
charge loss (12%)/recovery (30%) by cycling the
Table 3. Results from the cyclic voltammograms of PVF ﬁlms in aqueous based electrolytesa
NaCIO4 0.352 0.242 0.339 0.222
Figure 4. Cyclic voltammograms of PVF ﬁlms in aqueous based
electrolytes: (a) ﬁrst cycles; (b) 90th cycles.
Figure 5. Cyclic voltammograms of PVF ﬁlms showing the solvent effect
on charge density loss/recovery: (a) PVF ﬁlm cycled in PC/sulfolane/PC
LiClO4 electrolyte; (b) PVF ﬁlm cycled in sulfolane/PC/sulfolane TBAClO4
Poly(vinylferrocene) ®lms in electrolyte solutions
®lms in different solvent electrolyte independent of the
order of cycling, ie PC/sulfolane/PC or sulfolane/PC/
sulfolane and the salt used in the electrolyte. The
results summarized in Table 4 indicate that the
available charge density in a PVF ®lm depends
strongly on whether PC or sulfolane is the solvent.
We are not aware of such a strong `solvent effect'
having been reported previously. Further studies will
be extended to freshly prepared ®lms.
From the comparative studies of the PVF ®lms under
redox switching in aqueous and non-aqueous electro-
lyte solutions, the following conclusions can be drawn:
(i) the reversibility of the PVF ®lms in sulfolane-based
electrolytes was better than in PC and water; (ii) the
charge loss per cycle in sulfolane electrolytes was
smaller than in PC for the salts studied, ie LiClO4,
NaClO4, TBAClO4, TBABF4 and TBAPF6; (iii) after
90 cycles, there was no charge loss per cycle when
aqueous LiClO4 electrolyte was used; (iv) the ®lms
cycled in aqueous solution and in PC changed their
properties, particularly the charge density, much faster
than in sulfolane-based electrolytes; (v) there was a
reversible available charge decrease/increase caused by
cycling the ®lms between PC/sulfolane/PC; (vi) a
cycling between sulfolane/PC/sulfolane. This `solvent
sensitivity' effect, reported here for the ®rst time, has
important implications in choosing solvents for many
device applications of electroactive polymers. Future
work on the solvent effect will involve the study of the
conduction mechanisms of the same systems by using
It is important to understand the characteristics of
the PVF ®lms under redox switching for a proposed
application. Electroneutrality needs to be maintained
through the movement of electrolyte counterions in
and out of the ®lm with an associated solvent ¯ow by
the swelling/unswelling process.
High ionic conductivity, high boiling and low
melting points, and chemical stability are the most
desired electrolyte properties. Solutions of lithium
salts in PC have been used in battery and similar
applications as electrolytes and have proved to be
among the most ef®cient in terms of cyclability.
Nevertheless, it is known that electrolytes based on
PC could be a problem if lithium is used as anode
material. Sulfolane based electrolytes have shown to
be stable when used with modi®ed polythiophenl
electrodes22 and the results shown in this paper also
con®rm the high stability of these electrolytes when
used with PVF modi®ed electrodes.
High dielectric constant and low viscosity are
properties that enhance ionic conductivity because
they favour salt dissociation and high ionic diffusion
rates, respectively. The dielectric constant or relative
permittivity values of water, PC and sulfolane are 78.4,
64.9 and 44.0, respectively. For ions of normal size, no
ion pair formation occurs if the dielectric constant is
above about 40. Although, water and PC have higher
dielectric constants than sulfolane, the results shown
here suggest that the systems based on sulfolane are
more stable and more reversible than aqueous- and
PC-based electrolytes. One of the reasons for this
difference may be that sulfolane is a very weakly basic
solvent with a very feeble ability for solvating cations.
The ion±solvent, ion±ion and solvent±solvent inter-
actions are complex phenomena and are still not well
understood. Sulfolane may be a good solvent to be
used in electrochemical experiments, but the success-
ful use of aprotic solvents depends on high purity of
both the solvent and supporting electrolyte in a water-
